Row 44-45, Col 46-47: sub-cell (0,1) = g (gray)
Row 44-45, Col 48-49: sub-cell (0,2) = g (gray)

Row 46-47, Col 46-47: sub-cell (1,1) = R (red)

Row 48-49, Col 46-47: sub-cell (2,1) = g (gray)

W g g

g g W

Wait, maybe I need to look at this problem from a completely different angle. What if the bottom-left grid is NOT the input, and the top-left and top-right are examples of the transformation?

Actually, let me look at the colors more carefully. The top grids use b (blue) and R (red), while the bottom grids also use b and R. The centers use W (white), g (gray), and R (red).
What if the transformation rule is color-dependent? For example, maybe the rule is "swap the two cells that have different colors in positions (1,2) and (2,2)"?

sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current outer: b R b / R . b / b R R
# Target outer: R R b / b . b / b R R
# Need: (0,0) b->R, (0,1) R->R (ok), (0,2) b->b (ok), (1,0) R->b, (1,2) b->b (ok), (2,0) b->b (ok), (2,1) R->R (ok), (2,2) R->R (ok)
# Actually need: (0,0) b->R, (1,0) R->b

predict(expect_change=True, note='Set outer to R R b / b . b / b R R')
action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 38, 'col': 38},  # (0,0) b->R
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 46, 'col': 38},  # (1,0) R->b
])

lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("After setting outer:")
for r in [38, 46, 54]:
    c0 = lines[r][36:42]
    c1 = lines[r][44:50]
    c2 = lines[r][52:58]
    print(f"  {r}: {c0} {c1} {c2}")
print("Level completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed', False))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
